topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the job posting on his screen, the words swimming before his eyes like disturbed water. He hadn't really looked at the details in weeks; the anticipation had curdled into a heavy, inert feeling in his chest. He dragged a hand through his already messy hair, the movement slow and deliberate. Across the room, Maya was humming a tune, her energy a sharp, bright contrast to his own inertia. He felt a dull pressure behind his temples, a constant, low-grade fatigue that made even breathing feel like a conscious effort. When he finally clicked 'submit,' his fingers felt clumsy, heavy, as if weighted down by invisible anchors. He immediately leaned back in his chair, letting his shoulders slump further, wishing the floor would simply absorb him.

[story 2]

"So, you're really going for the Apex position?" Maya asked, her voice ringing with an almost frantic excitement. Elias nodded once, a minimal gesture that required surprising internal coordination. He felt a profound lack of drive, a vacancy where ambition used to reside. Every muscle in his body seemed to have decided to take an unscheduled vacation. He traced the condensation on his water glass, watching the droplets slowly merge and run down the cold surface. *Why bother?* the thought echoed in a hollow space. He glanced at the application form again, the ink blurring slightly under the harsh office light. He didn't feel hopeful; he felt merely… present, an observer in his own life, waiting for the next inevitable movement to happen, even if that movement was just the slow drift toward the next hour.

[story 3]

I kept rereading the job description, the text blurring into an indecipherable smear. My eyelids felt glued shut, making the simple act of reading an arduous physical struggle. There was a thick, heavy blanket draped over my motivation, suffocating any spark of desire. When Maya approached, radiating an almost alarming level of enthusiasm, I managed a weak, almost inaudible greeting. My gaze drifted toward the window, where the city traffic moved with an indifferent, relentless rhythm. I didn't want to be excited; I just wanted the weight to lift, to feel anything other than this pervasive sense of being thoroughly drained, like a battery that had been perpetually plugged in but never charged. I signed the document with a shaky hand, the pen moving with agonizing slowness.
